title: Chapter 33
text: Chapter Thirty R onin leaned against a concrete wall in the main chamber, watching the activity around him. He’d yet to determine the number of inhabitants with any certainty — two hundred and fifteen unique faces were catalogued in his memory, but there were many places he hadn’t accessed . The base was well-maintained, but it was lived-in. Total cleanliness wasn’t possible here. People worked, interacted, and played in the chambers and corridors. Conversations were frequent, and typically amiable. Children’s laughter echoed off the walls, nearly swallowed up in the din of activity . Cheyenne was in stark contrast to this place. The buildings and grounds of the Bot District were impeccably maintained, despite limited resources. Every house had intact windows, freshly-painted siding, and neatly trimmed lawns, hedges, and trees. The streets were free of debris. But there was no laughter, no warmth. Many of the residences within the wall were occupied, but the Bot District was as cold and silent as a tomb . The slums represented the opposite extreme. The humans there lived in squalor, in ramshackle buildings pieced together from whatever scrap was available. It was a place filled with people, yet somehow devoid of life. People sat near their doors and stared with dead eyes at passersby, surviving only because doing so was habit . Laughter caught Ronin’s attention now. He shifted his optics to a nearby group — ten children following a tall, box-torso bot. One of the children had written on the bot’s back with a piece of chalk . KICK ME At their laughter, the bot halted. The children stopped, too, failing to stifle their giggles with their raised hands. Slowly, the bot’s head turned around to look down at them with round eyes . “The tone of your amusement indicates mischief. Please explain so we may return to class immediately .” Ronin couldn’t be sure why — was it the way the bot’s head turned, the tone of its voice, or how it looked with its head on backwards? — but the children burst into fits of laughter. The bot’s head remained in place as its body ponderously tottered around to face them . “Explain. Or I will be forced to issue detention slips to all of you .” Another bot — a sleeker model, but not a synth — stopped behind the teacher. Its head tilted, and after a moment’s pause, it raised its foot and kicked the teacher’s rear end . Ronin couldn’t hold back his own smile as the children laughed uncontrollably. A few of them doubled over, holding their stomachs, and one even fell to the floor . The teacher rocked slightly as the other bot kicked it again. Slowly, the teacher spun its head to face behind . “You are disrupting the education of my students .” “I am only following instructions,” the other bot replied . “Desist your behavior or I will issue you a detention slip .” The bot tilted its head to the side and lowered its foot. A second later, it raised its other foot and kicked the teacher again . The teacher — managing a glare, despite its fixed facial features — turned its body with a few lumbering steps. “You have ignored your verbal warning. Printing detention slip .” There was a whirring noise from inside the teacher, but it stopped when a pair of soldiers approached. The students, red-faced, struggled to regain their composure . One of the soldiers wiped the chalk letters off the bot’s back with his sleeve and shot the kids a half-heartedly disapproving look. The other stepped between the two bots, grinning . “Come on, Terence. Mr. Mathers has class to get back to. You don’t have to follow every sign the kids stick on his back .” “You are correct. But it is more fun to do so.” Terence leaned to the side, glancing at the children, and the shutter on one of his optics slipped closed before he walked away. The kids giggled again as Mr. Mathers and the two soldiers turned to face them . “Detention slips for everyone. Printing—ERROR! SPOOL OUT OF PAPER! BLACK INK LOW! CANCELLING PRINT .” “How many times have we told you kids to stop playing pranks on Mr. Mathers ?” The children, as one, glanced down at their shoes . “Listen to him, because he’s got more knowledge than you can begin to understand. He’ll teach you everything you need to know, just like he taught us, and your moms and dads and grandparents .” “Now what do you say to Mr. Mathers?” the other soldier asked . “We’re sorry,” the children said together . “Due to lack of ink and paper, your apology is accepted. We must return immediately to the classroom to resume instruction.” Mr. Mathers turned and walked away . As the children shuffled behind him, one of the soldiers crouched beside a student . “Hey, here’s what you should write the next time …” The soldier’s hushed words were lost in the din of activity as the group moved away . Ronin couldn’t recall witnessing anything like it during his travels. There were places where humans and bots coexisted peacefully — most anywhere outside of Cheyenne, really — but nowhere else where their lives were so integrated. This was what Newton meant. This was how humans and bots were designed to interact . He pushed off the wall and walked toward his quarters; it was time to wake Lara. In the eight days since their first heated exchange, Rodriguez hadn’t attempted to question her again. The Colonel was done waiting . At least she’d forgiven Ronin for the laudanum, even if that forgiveness came with a promise to remove a few of his parts if he did it again . The base’s leaders had been generous enough — at Nancy’s insistence, most likely — to provide he and Lara with a room outside the infirmary. It was sparsely furnished, with only a double bed, a small table, and a footlocker, but it had a working door and was warm and dry. More than they’d hoped for, upon leaving Cheyenne . Lara was already awake when he entered their room. She smiled up at him, and he couldn’t help noticing the light in her eyes. The swelling was nearly gone, and most of her bruises had faded to yellow . “Feel better after your nap?” he asked . Her smile faded. “I couldn’t sleep. Not looking forward to being inter…intro…what was the word ?” “ Interrogated .” “Yeah. That. I don’t know what the hell he thinks he’s going to get out of me.” Lara rose slowly, her movements stiff. She could walk around without help now, but the subtle changes to her expression often belied her discomfort. She adjusted the lay of her sling around her neck. “And just because I’m cooled down doesn’t mean I’m not still pissed, or that I won’t blow up on them again .” “Make an effort, at least, to think about things from their side .” “I have, and I get it. I would’ve done a lot to protect Tabitha if I had the chance. But even at my lowest, when I didn’t know where my next meal was coming from, I still helped people out. Because that’s how things are supposed to be. We’re not…we’re not supposed to see other people suffering and just look away, pretending nothing happened .” “They inherited this situation, Lara. They were born into it .” “And I was born to die under Warlord’s boot?” she countered. “All those people there, they only exist to be his entertainment? You’ve got all those bots who follow his lead because it’s too dangerous not to, all those humans living in terror…and the whole time, two miles away, there were enough soldiers with enough firepower to end him, and all these people did was hide. They might as well have been the ones who left us there to die .” “They didn’t leave you, Lara,” Ronin said. His processors hung up on the thought of her being left to die, nearly calling up images he didn’t want to see again . “You crossed Warlord,” she said, stepping close. “You risked yourself to protect me. Why? Why did you go out of your way to bury Tabitha? You’re supposed to operate on logic or some shit, and everything you’ve done since I met you is the total opposite .” “I did those things because I love you .” Something in her expression softened. She placed her hand on his chest, fingers curling in his coat. “You did things before you fell in love with me. Before you even knew what love was. What was your reason then ?” His processors fired through all the memories of his time with her. “You intrigued me. And…you seemed lost. On the verge of collapsing .” “You helped me at my lowest point. Maybe some of your reasons were selfish, but not all of them. You could’ve tried to demand so much of me, could’ve turned me into your slave. And even before you loved me, you gave me food, and shelter, and safety, and asked for so little in return .” “Because I had an abundance of those things to offer,” he replied. As he spoke the words, the final piece clicked into place, and he understood her anger . It must’ve shown on his face, because she smiled up at him softly and rested her forehead on his chest. “Warlord needs to be stopped, Ronin .” “I don’t think these people will risk it, Lara, but I’ll help you try to convince them all the same .” “Thank you.” She lifted her head, stood on her toes, and pressed her lips to his. He leaned into the kiss, an electric thrill pulsing over his skin. Lara wrapped her right arm around him and squeezed, only to suck in a sharp breath and step back. Lines of strain appeared around her mouth, despite her smirk. “You’d think I would remember how much that hurts. Guess I just can’t resist .” Ronin brushed the backs of his fingers over her cheek. She was still recovering, still delicate, and he missed holding her, missed feeling her body tucked against his, missed her warmth. His memories of those feelings were easily accessed, but could never compare to the real thing . He took her hand in his, and they walked together, slowly, to the room he’d been questioned in when they first arrived . Lara was quiet as they entered the stockade. Though she’d probably never seen anything like it, Ronin got the sense that she knew what the cells were for. She gave them a single glance before diverting her gaze to the concrete floor . The interrogation room — he was sure that’s what it had been, even before the Black Out — was full when Ronin and Lara walked in. They sat together in the only open seats, at the end of the table nearest the door. The base’s leadership stared at them, Colonel Rodriguez central amongst the group . Lara didn’t release Ronin’s hand . “Miss Brooks,” Rodriguez said, dipping his head slightly. “I imagine you’re acquainted with everyone here, by now .” Lara glanced around the room. “Yeah. Hi, Newton.” She raised a hand in greeting . Newton mimicked the gesture with surprising fluidity, face plates lifting into a smile. “Lara. You’re looking well .” Rodriguez cleared his throat. “We’re not here for small talk. You grew up in Cheyenne, Brooks. That means I have questions, and you’re going to answer them .” “You know, you technically all grew up in Cheyenne, too. So…you can answer them yourself .” Several of the people around the table grinned, many of whom quickly hid their mouths behind their hands . Rodriguez’s features darkened. “Stand-up comedy died when the bombs dropped .” “I don’t know what that is .” “There were people called comedians who—” Newton began, but he was silenced by a glare from the Colonel . “Look, Miss Brooks, I’m going to be frank with you. I’d appreciate it if you could cut your shit .” She opened her mouth to respond, and Ronin gave her hand a warning squeeze; he could imagine what might have come out of her mouth, rich with ‘ f ’s and hard consonants . Lara looked at Ronin and pressed her lips together. She took a slow breath, and turned back to Rodriguez. “Ronin already told you more than I ever could about Cheyenne. His mind holds every detail. Unless you plan on going after Warlord, I don’t have anything to say to you .” Multiple conversations began at once . “Enough!” Rodriguez’s gaze roamed over the others, one-at-a-time. “You’re all here as a formality. This is a matter of security, and by our laws that means I am in sole command .” The heavy silence lasted for twenty-two seconds before Rodriguez returned his attention to Lara. “Warlord has been a potential threat to this facility for decades.” He splayed his hands on the table with deliberate slowness. “Any information we can gather about him may be vital to our continued safety and functionality. But I am curious, Miss Brooks, as to why you think we’d risk our people to go after him. He gave up searching this area many years ago, and hasn’t caused us trouble since .” “Why would you not?” she asked. “If there was a wild dog living next door to you, and every now and then it killed one of your neighbor’s kids, would you just ignore it because it hadn’t done anything to you ?” “Warlord hasn’t killed any of our neigh — ” Lara pulled her hand from Ronin’s and slammed it on the table, pushing herself up. “We’re your fucking neighbors !” Ronin scanned the people with his optics; their faces bore signs of shock and shame . “We’ve been your neighbors a long fucking time, and humans and bots are both suffering because of that bastard .” “Sit down,” Rodriguez said through his teeth . “ Fu — ” “We should hear her out,” William interjected . Seconds felt like years as Rodriguez and Lara glared at one another across the table. Ronin’s fingers twitched. The tension in the air was nearly thick enough for his skin sensors to detect. If it came down to it, he’d fight for her without hesitation . “I told you, Anderson, you’re here as a formality,” Rodriguez said, glancing at the scientist . “We’ve heard stories from our scouts over the years, but they’ve always been just that.” William held out a hand toward Lara, palm-up. “This young lady has experienced it firsthand. She’s suffered because of what our ancestors did .” “Everyone over there has suffered because of it,” Nancy added . “I’ve seen with my own optics what he is capable of.” Newton leaned forward, frowning as he looked at Lara. “I was sent out there, into that Wasteland, by your forbears to reactivate more of my kind. The goal was to work toward repairing the world, toward rebuilding civilization. You’ve all lost that vision as much as I had. This woman — who is braver than anyone in this room— has brought us an opportunity to begin what we were always meant to do .” Ronin looked at Lara. Her cheeks reddened, and she dropped her gaze as she settled into her seat. When she took his hand again, her grip was clammy and tight. The outburst had cost her . “The situation has changed,” said Dave Elliot. He was the quartermaster, in charge of the provisions within the base. The man who’d argued Lara was consuming too many resources. “Maybe a hundred years ago, we could’ve gone out and done something, but we have too many problems of our own now. We have to worry about our people before we risk everything for strangers .” “Every time I send people out, there’s a strong chance they won’t all come back,” the Colonel said, “and that’s just running for scrap and supplies .” Lara stared at him. “So, instead, you live in hiding. You cower down here, away from the world, because you’re too afraid to face Warlord .” Rodriguez surged to his feet, slamming his fists down on the table. Heavy as it was, the entire thing shook. Ronin stood as well. He’d face deactivation — or worse — to protect Lara . “Every person that’s died under my watch is another I have to carry with me forever! I can ask them to risk their lives for our own, for our survival, to keep their friends and family safe. But you want me to put all of our people at risk to go to war on behalf of some strangers ?” “What will you do when there are too many of you to fit in this place?” Lara asked, surprisingly calm. “When you’re so packed in that you can’t keep up with supplies and food? You’re trapped down here, and he’s as much your master as he is Cheyenne’s if you won’t go out and face this .” Lara rose, hand on the table for support, eyes steady on Rodriguez. “Will you deny couples children and kill their babies? Will you murder anyone who doesn’t follow the rules? Will you refuse someone food, because they aren’t valuable enough to you ?” “Our crops are struggling more and more. It’s a miracle they’ve lasted this long,” said Nancy. “A few more generations, and they won’t be viable .” “We have other plans in place to—” Dave began . “It may be decades before we have the breakthroughs to even begin that kind of genetic modification,” Will said. “Our children and grandchildren may not have that much time .” “There are resources in Cheyenne,” Ronin offered, not looking away from Rodriguez, who stared down at the tabletop. “Warlord has kept a number of fields producing crops, in addition to cattle and goats. Plus, there are production facilities on the south side of town that are still in some working order .” “With those, we can — ” “Enough,” Rodriguez commanded. The room fell silent again as he lowered himself into his seat, nostrils flared . After several moments, Lara sat down. Ronin followed suit. There was a defeated look on her face . Seconds ticked away on Ronin’s internal clock, stretching over a minute . “Is that what he’s doing over there?” Rodriguez finally asked, looking up at Lara . “ What ?” “Killing babies. Murdering anyone that doesn’t obey. Letting people starve. Is that what Warlord is doing in Cheyenne ?” She nodded. “We’re his playthings. He keeps us around until we’re not interesting anymore, or we’re too much trouble. We need his permission to have children, and if a couple has more than one…the baby is killed. He doesn’t allow anyone to grow enough food to survive. The entire settlement has to share a single water pump, and he shuts off the flow at his whim .” Lara found Ronin’s hand again, twining their fingers. “Relationships between humans and bots are forbidden. My sister was taken in by a synth, and when he found out about it, he beat her to death and tore the bot to pieces. He found out about me and Ronin not long after, and hunted us across the Dust like animals.” She took a steadying breath and squeezed Ronin’s hand. “There is no hope in Cheyenne. Only him .” “This is not something we have the resources to fix, Jack,” said Dave . For a long while, Rodriguez scrutinized Lara. “It’s not something we can afford to ignore any more, David,” he finally said . “They’re already using our resources without any sort of payment. Already burdening our people. Now we’re going to risk our lives for them? That’s not the kind of decision that’s going to keep this place running through the years .” “If there’s a viable alternative to this place,” said Captain Cooper, leaning forward beside Nancy, “it’s something we need to consider. The Andersons say we’re on limited time as it is, and all of us know people aren’t meant to live in holes like this .” “We need sunlight,” Nancy added. “Even the few of us that go out don’t get enough. The UV lamps don’t cut it, and they won’t last forever. The elderly are brittle, and too many of the young show signs of rickets .” “Will your people fight, Miss Brooks? Will they lay down their lives for a chance at being free of Warlord?” Rodriguez asked . “I can’t speak for them, but I think if they’re given a chance, if they’re given just a little hope...they would. They have nothing else .” “And the bots?” Rodriguez’s eyes flickered to Ronin . “I don’t know. Most are what you’d consider civilians, and I cannot guess the nature of their programming. Warlord’s lackeys will fight for him, but beyond that, I don’t know with any degree of certainty. Though …” Ronin shifted his optics to Newton. The first voice Ronin heard after reactivation, the source of his oldest uncorrupted memory... “If anyone could convince them, it would be the Prophet .” The plates over Newton’s optics rose high as the room’s attention fell upon him. “The stories told about me as the Prophet are exaggerated,” he said, a faint shakiness in his voice. “It borders on superstitious nonsense — ” “You’re the one who woke them up,” William said. “As bots have developed emotions over the years, that’s only become more significant to them. Stories are powerful things, whether we’re flesh or metal .” “But to think that I’d have any special sway over anyone else...I don’t — ” “Brooks, are you willing to speak to your people? To rally as many to the fight as you can?” Rodriguez asked . “I’ll do what I can,” Lara said . He nodded and turned to Newton. “She’s going to risk everything, again, because she thinks this is the right thing to do. What about you? Will you talk to the bots in Cheyenne ?” Newton frowned. “I...yes. Yes, I will. Illogical as it may be to walk into the den of our enemy, stand up in the middle of the square, and incite a riot, I will .” “Lara is still wounded,” Ronin said, “and any gearhead that’s seen her would shoot on sight. She can’t go back to Cheyenne .” Lara turned to him. “I need to do this, Ronin .” “Anyone else can do it,” he replied. “I need you safe. Alive .” She pulled her hand free and cupped his jaw. “I can’t ask them to sacrifice and not be willing to do the same. I know the people there, and they know me.” Leaning forward, she pressed her forehead to his. “We can’t let Warlord go on like this .” His processors calculated hundreds, thousands, millions of possibilities. Countless ways in which it could all go wrong, countless ways for him to lose her again. Permanently. But he knew her well enough to know he wouldn’t win this fight. He took her face in his hands and kissed her, shutting down the calculations, pushing aside his worry . “She’s to be as far away from any fighting as possible,” Ronin said after he pulled away, turning to Rodriguez . The Colonel nodded. “We’ll make a plan based on the map you drew for us. For now, I’m sure we’re all fed up with this meeting. We’ll reconvene tomorrow morning to begin preparations.” Rodriguez raised a hand, silencing Dave Elliot before the man could get out a word. “ Dismissed .”
